name: Q-ball (non-topological soliton) dark matter
summary: Supersymmetric extensions of the Standard Model generically allow stable non-topological solitons — Q-balls — carrying conserved charge (baryon, lepton, or other U(1)) and stabilised by the structure of the scalar potential. Q-balls form copiously in the early universe via Affleck-Dine fragmentation along flat directions and can constitute dark matter while simultaneously generating the baryon asymmetry.

addresses_puzzles.note: Macroscopic Q-balls (with charge Q ~ 10^20 - 10^25) form during Affleck-Dine baryogenesis along MSSM flat directions; relic abundance set by AD field initial conditions. Distinctive direct-detection signatures (slow-moving macroscopic objects passing through detectors) constrained by SLIM and CDMS.
Q-ball formation occurs simultaneously with Affleck-Dine baryogenesis; the dark matter and baryon asymmetry have a common origin.

touches_benchmarks.note: Q-ball signatures are slow-moving 'tracks' rather than nuclear recoils; standard WIMP analyses don't apply. Custom searches set specific bounds.

new_scales: Q-ball formation scale ~ inflaton VEV / MSSM flat direction
new_symmetries: U(1) charge conservation (baryon or lepton number)
introduces.note: Builds on MSSM. Free parameters are the Affleck-Dine field initial conditions, Q-ball charge distribution, and SUSY-breaking soft mass.

history: Coleman (1985) introduced Q-balls as non-topological solitons. Kusenko (1997), 'Q-Ball as Dark Matter', PLB 405, 108. Kusenko, Shaposhnikov (1998), 'Supersymmetric Q-balls as dark matter', Phys. Lett. B 418, 46, arXiv:hep-ph/9709492. Detailed phenomenology developed through 2000s; remains a viable alternative to particle DM.

references.citation: Kusenko and Shaposhnikov, 'Supersymmetric Q-balls as dark matter', Phys. Lett. B 418, 46 (1998)
